speaker: interviewee
text: And the place where I was sitting on one side, [INAUDIBLE] on one side [INAUDIBLE]. Across from us, there were women sitting. And then I saw my mother sitting. And next to my mother was my sister. My sister was 16 years old. My mother was 44 years old. They were sitting there on the ground. And then later on it, they picked up some men with good muscles. They're specialists. They took us 120 men and they put us as [INAUDIBLE] in the yard, not far. And they laid on the ground with the head down to the ground. And that's why I didn't see anymore. But when all the people start marching out, I picked my head out and I saw that they were marching six abreast. And for both sides, there was a general or a policeman [INAUDIBLE] dogs. Each one had a-- and they were all marching away. It was 8 o'clock in the morning. At that time, I lost my mother and my system, my grandpa, and the grandpa survive. And that time was two and a half thousand people. After that, [INAUDIBLE] make a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what happened to your family then or later?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know 12 o'clock the same day, came in horse and wagon-- horse and wagon with clubbing. And they took from our people with 120 us and saved us. Took some 20 people, unload the clothing. When they came back to us, they said we just unload the clothing from the people where they were killed. So we know the same day, 12 o'clock, they were killing people

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you? What Did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, I was not crying. But when I came home in the evening, I cried very much. And all the feathers was out. All the-- you know, they were look-- [INAUDIBLE] in the feathers, we have good. So they came to us, everything taken away. And that time, I cried for three days with-- didn't even go to look for [INAUDIBLE] places to [INAUDIBLE]. We didn't eat for two or three days, me and my father. And after 30 days, he said, we have no choice. We have to go together. [INAUDIBLE] up and go together. But that day, I did not cry. A lot of people, they were running around us running-- you know, [INAUDIBLE] running. They're scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- you had to pick up the hand and they would allow them to get up and run next yard [INAUDIBLE]. But not me. I was like a stone. I didn't that time. Only when I came home then I start crying. Not one day-- the three days. They had taken-- ransacked the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they ransacked the house. They took everything away. The same neighborhood-- the same neighbor ran into us, he took everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I know? I had a second cousin from the Russian side, saved himself and came in here. And when [INAUDIBLE] he went in on the attic [? of us. ?] And he saved himself on the attic in our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he looked through. And he saw our neighbor taking things from our house away. And then after that, what-- we didn't have neither choice. We have to go to ghetto. We had all the furniture. We went in. We told him, take the furniture. Take everything whatever you take. We didn't say anything. But later on, when I was hiding, [INAUDIBLE] an [? atonement. ?] I came and he had a Russian coat. And he [INAUDIBLE]. He says, I'm going to get killed anyway. I told him, we gave the furniture and you took yourself everything. And you're not sure, but they are right. So he wants to give me back. And he grab me, I run out. [INAUDIBLE]. What did you learn happened to your mother and sister and grandfather. They took them. The women-- some of them couldn't walk. They give them a horse and wagon. They take him. And the rest then, the younger ones are walking. They're walking five miles. It was a little village, [? Cossack. ?] And they had a little wood there. In the woods, they made the two graves-- separate for men, separate for woman. And they made steps. And [INAUDIBLE] was staying up an SS man with a machine gun and was going. They were naked. 10 people down. And he shoot them all. The next 10, shoot them all. And the same thing for the women, another SS man. They were all drunk, all drinking a lot of whisky. And as a matter of fact, the graves right now-- last year, 50 people from Israel, from the United States, and from Canada and Toronto, they make a fence, iron fence. [INAUDIBLE] $1,000 each from [INAUDIBLE] contributors. And they made a fence around. They put more ground on top. The bones shouldn't stick out. It's not sticking out and now cows going there. This [INAUDIBLE]. Matter of fact, I have a tape from that [INAUDIBLE] went there. They made speeches and [INAUDIBLE] I think there they made up [INAUDIBLE]. And then the other two places, they find out [INAUDIBLE] made some other ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: A memorial?

speaker: interviewee
text: A fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: A memorial?

speaker: interviewee
text: A memorial and a fence. So we have a memorial twice a year. We have Warsaw memorial. We have [INAUDIBLE] memorial. They made a fence now. [INAUDIBLE] at least bones covered up. And it's fenced in. Nobody goes in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you what had happened at the time? How did you learn it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean, how was this-- I was there. I was there. And so people, like I told you-- [INAUDIBLE] came in. Some other people saved themselves from the grave. They were on their way. Because two and a half thousand peopels-- was a mishmash. Some of them run away from them. Some of them were shooting. They were dead. And then they wake up. They were half dead. At night, we came back to our city. So [INAUDIBLE]. So we knew everything was going on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you and your father then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what affect me. I said I don't believe in human beings. I'm not a believe [INAUDIBLE] religious because we are not [INAUDIBLE]. Like I told you, the rabbis, some did very bad. In [INAUDIBLE], I didn't do good to the [INAUDIBLE] then. I am not too much a believer and I believe because-- we were killed because we were not organized. And the rabbis and the-- mostly the rich guys didn't organize us. And that's why we went to the graves without any fighting. And I'm disappointed in god. Because the best people went in the grave. And someone people were-- they were thieves. They were traitors. They were all kinds of stuff. They've saved themselves at least, maybe a year or so. But later on, they were killed. But they saved themselves. But the intelligent, the learners, they went first.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's almost 50 years from that experience. Do you have-- is there a message or a lesson that you would like to leave for the world or your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the world, a message that you have to understand that every human being is worth something. And it cannot be killed. And every nation has to be organized. The best-- the worst [INAUDIBLE] prejudice is they were egoist. Each one is for himself. And that's no good. You have to be organized together and fight together. If you're by yourself and you're all about you, then it's no good. Then you get killed. That's what I believe in Israel because they are of a government there. And they're organized there and they say it's not going to be any more like that. And I'm against the religious people because the religious people, they're fanatic, and they're not organized. They believe in God and they're not organizing the Jewish nation like supposed to be a nation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel [? Corets ?] was not organized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I feel we were-- we didn't have no letters. We didn't nobody to tell us anything what to do. And that's why we went one time, four or five times, to kill us. And everybody was thinking about himself and not [INAUDIBLE]. That's why we were killed. It shouldn't be like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the first experience that you had, considering hearing that you had lived in a very Jewish city, what was the first sign of anti-semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I was born, we used to fight the gentile. Because we were around-- our town was at river in three sides. We threw stones on the other side of the river, they threw stones to us. Because they teach them that the Jews killed Jesus. And it was a big-- they didn't like us. We didn't have no friends-- very few. A few of them [INAUDIBLE] because they say, like I say, I was going to run. Some of them tried the same. Some of them tried to run after me with axe. I want to tell you a story. One time, I was [INAUDIBLE] woman. I went hungry. I'm going to beg. And all of a sudden, a big snow coming in. And they cannot see anything. It was dark and I [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in the house. I says, can I warm up a little bit? He says the farmer, OK, warm up. And I'm warming up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I [INAUDIBLE] myself. I want to go. He says to me, you can stay overnight here. Good, I'll stay overnight then. I stay there. and a couple minutes later, three guys walking in. Fancy, dressed up with shoes, beautiful high boots with hats, beautiful. They're looking around. One of the three was a boyfriend friend to a girl now. He coming to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They say, well, who are you? I says I'm a prisoner. [INAUDIBLE] prison, they let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Show the documents. I show them some kind of documents. All right. They notice that I'm Jewish. He says, oh, we need like you. Let's go. He takes me by the arm. Three guys-- one on the arm, the other guy-- we go out. We're going out. Then the boyfriend from-- he's still talking to the father. And two are holding it. And we went into the yard. End of the yard-- that guy doesn't go after us. So one guy says to the other, go on. Take him, take him.

speaker: interviewee
text: What is-- take him-- so dark, pitch dark. Go take him. What you're standing there? So one guy is left and he holds my arm. I pick my [INAUDIBLE] up and give them in the nose. And he lets go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm running. He runs after me. I'm running dark in the field. He runs after me. I hear him running. Then I fall down a ditch. He didn't see it. He run after me. And I pick myself up and I ran the other way. And that's the way I [? save him. ?] I find out the three guys-- one was collecting milk for the Germans. One was a secretary for the Germans too. Later, a week later, they caught a couple of Jewish guys and they took them to a police station. I, at that time, had ruin away.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you think was the first sign of danger for your community, for your family? First time when they SS came in but four weeks came in. And a German, a [INAUDIBLE], all the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came in and said, bad people came in here. Watch, I tell my mother. Watch out for you men.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm staying there. Then all of a sudden, I work at the yard to clean up some parts. A German came in, passing by the street. So I'm picking up the parts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says I'm working for Germans for the [INAUDIBLE]. And he thought this through. He let me go. And that time, the SS young guy said, well, beating up all Jews. They're not killing them. It was a beating up day. They caught my friend. He told me the story. They caught him.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took him where they take up for the-- what do you call this? From the ground, they take up water. You take out the water there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He takes the water and he puts on him. And they're all laughing. Maybe a half an hour, all-- and this was October then-- until you fall down. Until they fall down and they're laughing, they let him go. They throw him in the [INAUDIBLE]. So that time was the first time the dangers I noticed that-- that time, they didn't kill. There was four weeks and they're beating up-- the SS beating up. They were passing by the street. [INAUDIBLE] so let's have beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of your other experiences during the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other experience of the occupation is [INAUDIBLE] like I told you, we had the clothing. We borrowed it. And  we was very angry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I was working in the sugar factor, the farm would bring in root for the sugar factory. They pay me with molasses. Then no one molasses left-- was on the bottom of the sugar. Was sitting down, which does not run. So he has to go-- the tank is like a big house. He has to go to the latter up And down inside to collect this molasses. So I am begging the farmer-- I will go for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go upstairs for the buckets, go down into the bucket inside, collect [INAUDIBLE] molasses. The meantime, I had a piece of clothing, putting up a little bit sugar molasses on my belt for myself, save it. And then in the evening, I take the piece of molasses and I give to some other guy. He gave me a piece of bread or maybe three or four potatoes. That's the life of us in the winter 19-- in the winter '41, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here are some pictures of your family and your life in town. Would you show them to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you left it a little higher up so we can see?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was me in-- for the ration already. I never had a picture. So I was in a big time [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I made a picture of myself. This is the river. Same name [INAUDIBLE] river. In the background, we see a chimney from the sugar factory. So maybe a half kilometer, they have the sugar factor there.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have big land was going through there, the river there. We were swimming there The river goes around our town, around the town from all three sides. And the use of a good time was fresh air and the sand [INAUDIBLE] farther out a little bit. And that was for the town. Everybody was going swimming. Of course, there were separate swimming for women, separate swimming for men. But we were swimming there and jumping and every-- mostly around Saturday, Sunday, we had a good time a little bit past that river father out and same river. That's what I remember of that river.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had a very good time. It was not too many fish, but the fresh air-- and we enjoyed the-- the young people enjoyed the life around the river. You want to see next picture? That?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a picture of my brother, younger brother. He was born in 1924. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. In Hebrew, it was [INAUDIBLE]. He was killed in our language in [INAUDIBLE]. That was the 8th of August. He was-- he helped out in a magazine for selling shoes. It was there. And 8th of August, he was helping my neighbor, a Christian guy. He was making an open. There was another brick layer with his son. He went there too. He was helping with the oven. And somebody says that there are three Jews there. And the Ukrainian police came in. They took him away. And that day, the 8th of August, they took away 450 people from our [INAUDIBLE]. They took them about seven miles from our town in a ditch. They threw grenade at them and they kill them all. Of course at that time, I didn't know, you know-- later on in the story. But [INAUDIBLE] my brother. OK. This is a picture of me in my father. In 1938, my father in the full time was buying a lot of [INAUDIBLE]. They used [INAUDIBLE] for our [INAUDIBLE] and we used to sell [INAUDIBLE] out to the bigger cities. We put the [INAUDIBLE] in the barrels and we sent to big city on horse and wagon. And the biggest city was named Rivne. At that time, Rivne, in September 1938 was a big show-- a show. They have all kind of selling goods and then they show a lot of stuff. I'm begging my father I want to go there. That was 60 miles from our town. But to go there would cost $4. My father couldn't afford $4. The foreman, the guy what takes the [INAUDIBLE], says he can go on the horse and wagon. Horse and wagon would take him. 20 hours to go. My father allowed me [INAUDIBLE] on the bells. I sit on the bells. I was riding the horse and wagon all night. At 12 o'clock night, I was very tired, sleepy. I took out my belt. When-- I took the belt and I bend it through the strings. And that's the way I slept all night. I fall down, I just wake up again. And I was going on the horse and wagon all night, 60 miles, to the town Rivne. And when I came to Rivne, I met my father like a business man. He went with the boss. He paid the $4. I met him there. And on the street was photographer. So we made a picture of me and my father in 1938 of September. I don't know exactly the date is. And of course, I was very surprised. At first time my life I went to big city-- 20,000 people. They show us the wall of death.

speaker: interviewee
text: That means a big bell. And a guy was riding a motorcycle up, down-- up and down the bell. The first time I see something like that-- a big bell, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then people throwing money in that because nobody want to [? short ?] him. Then we saw a little bit animals. It was the first time in my life I saw this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the date I made that picture of me and my father. This is the picture of me for-- that time was Russians. I meant to Lemberg. This was 250 miles from us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They call it Lemberg town, a big town. And I was working for the Russians. I was collecting all kind of goods for the sugar factory. And I had a few hours left. I went in to a photographer. And I was-- that time, they had dress up like human beings. So I went into a photographer and he made that picture of me in 1944 around March-- that was the month of March. I don't know exactly the date what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else about that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, I worked for the Russians. They had a place in Lemberg to collect all kinds of stuff for the sugar factor. The need alcohol. We need the belts. We need some filters for the-- so I used to get it, collect it by a machine going back to [INAUDIBLE]. Machine-- was driving the machine. And wood, little pieces of wood under pressure, was producing gas. And it was done with this kind of machine. Going back to Lemberg, we used to have in our town where-- the sugar factory had gardens.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had potato, [INAUDIBLE], all kinds. So they collect this in the sacks and we send that to big shots in Lemberg. So we give them that. And then back, we needed [INAUDIBLE] and they had to supply it. So they give us supplies. And I used to sign for that, for the [INAUDIBLE], for all kinds of stuff [INAUDIBLE] back. And I had a few hours left. So I went in. I made a picture of myself. Describe what you're wearing. Describe your clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was civilian clothes. But I was-- for Russians, I bought-- it was very cold. So I bought it from the soldiers. The coats, the [INAUDIBLE] coats-- one coat wasn't enough. I wore two. I wore two courts of me. And of course, they have to watch them-- the stuff. So I was sitting. Took us a whole day to go from Lemberg to [? coats. ?] So I was sitting on top of them on an open truck and watching myself nobody should steal it. And then sometimes at night, we had to-- in order to start the car, the truck, we have to stay on the hill and warm up the oil with fire, put in hot water, and then go down the hill and it start. We didn't have a starter. I start cranking up. It was very hard in the meantime.

speaker: interviewee
text: So going down the hill and then the machine starts. And then you never could-- you have to run the machine all day until we come to [INAUDIBLE]. None of us [INAUDIBLE] should we stop, we cannot start again the machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Nathan, go ahead. This is a teacher from-- Yeah. [INAUDIBLE]. After the war, we had a very famous partisan [INAUDIBLE]. He was an engineer. And he wrote a book about codes wherever it happened. The book is 1,500 pages. And wherever he finds the pictures, he puts in the book. That's me over there. That's me. That's a picture. I belong to a Zionist organization [INAUDIBLE]. And they made a picture of me in-- some of them was here [? before ?] the people. We only about five people still alive. This was the [INAUDIBLE]. He was the older one. And we had a good time in [INAUDIBLE]. We have [INAUDIBLE] long [? call ?] kind of lectures. And we had dentists, [INAUDIBLE] dentist. So this reminds me of my teenage years where I had a good time there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Nathan?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my graduation from the Polish school. They made We graduate, we made our picture. And I'm here over there. And down the first row, this is our teachers. We have teachers, about six teachers. And this was a graduation. And I was a graduate-- not too many graduates because it was very hard. And I made it. And this was in 1936. I graduated. And this was 1936 in the end of May. OK. This is a picture where I graduate. And down below is our teachers. The first row, this picture here, he was teaching us languages in German. [INAUDIBLE]. He was Jewish and he survived. Next was our teacher who made us, for the girls, to [INAUDIBLE] work, Lieberman. I don't know if he survived or not. There's the third one, was principal-- Glass. He was a Polish officer. He was a small guy and he survived. The Russians sent him to Siberia. He survived Siberia Barry and he came back to Israel. He survived. Next-- his name was Glass. Next is a teacher, Margulis. I don't know if he survived or not. Next is teacher, a Ukrainian teacher. He was a nationalist. He was working with the Germans, but he saw that he killed a lot of people. And it [INAUDIBLE] bad. So give away-- he give away the job. He didn't want to work in our town. [INAUDIBLE] his name is. Next is a teacher from religion-- so [INAUDIBLE]. He was killed too when they killed 2 and 1/2 thousand people. He was a very nice, sweet man. He had very educated son and a daughter. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. Then next [INAUDIBLE] was a girl from [INAUDIBLE] same thing. She was not a teacher for both sides. And this one from the other side too was a pupil like me, staying in school and finishing school. This is my graduation picture. And I finished high school. I [INAUDIBLE] possibly to go higher. Then [INAUDIBLE] and two years later, three years later, when the Russians came in, they gave a possibility to learn. I went back into school to study. I was trying to be an engineer. But the war came in and I was lost. Cut.
